# FRAMEWORK PDF CAPTIONS — ken-drought 2023-02-19
# framework_doc: https://reliefweb.int/report/kenya/kenya-drought-early-action-protocol-summary-eap2022ke02
# pages: 14 · model: sonnet · via: claude headless
# Auto-generated descriptions of the PDF's visuals (maps/charts/tables). PUBLIC.
------------------------------------------------------------
--- page 1 ---
Cover page: IFRC Early Action Protocol Summary — Kenya, drought. Date: 15.02.2022. EAP No: EAP2022KE02, EAP timeframe: 5 Years, MDR Code not shown, EAP approved: 11/10/2022, Early action timeframe: 7 Months. Budget: CHF 499,199. Target: 150,000 people. Photo shows relief food distribution to 500 families in Samburu County by Kenya Red Cross.

--- page 2 ---
Map: Kenya county-level Drought Risk Index choropleth. Northern and northeastern counties shaded darkest (highest drought risk index, legend shows gradient from light ~0.11 to dark ~0.80+). Counties in ASAL (Arid and Semi-Arid Lands) region — including Turkana, Marsabit, Wajir, Mandera, Samburu — are highest risk. Southern/coastal counties are lighter (lower risk). Main point: drought risk is concentrated in northern/ASAL Kenya. Narrative context: DREF approved CHF 499,199 (CHF 135,978 readiness/pre-positioning + CHF 363,222 early actions). Kenya has two rainy seasons: MAM (long rains) and OND (short rains); 80% of Kenya is ASAL with rainfall 200–500 mm/year.

--- page 3 ---
Bar chart — Top 10 Reported Drought Impacts (horizontal bars, x-axis = Frequency, range ~0–250). Bars in descending order: Food insecurity/food productivity (~250), Malnutrition/loss of livelihoods (~200), Crop failure (~180), Disease outbreaks (~150), Education (~100), Water shortage/degradation (~90), Conflict/insecurity (~80), Poor wash standards (~60), Production/gender issues (~40), Displacement (~30). Main point: food insecurity and malnutrition are the dominant reported impacts of drought in Kenya (1973–2022). High drought risk counties: Turkana, Marsabit, Wajir, Samburu, Mandera, Tana River; also eastern/southern counties Makueni, Kwale.

--- page 4 ---
Narrative text, no figure. Trigger mechanism: EAP triggered for 23 ASAL counties when KMD OND forecast SPI value ≤ -0.98 with ≥33% chance of occurrence in at least 3 of 23 counties. Lead time: up to 12 weeks; early actions implementation starts in July. Trigger process: KMD issues OND forecast → drought risk map updated by IBF dashboard using new forecast info and IPC phase projections → counties ranked by order of risk index → top 3 counties classified as alert/normal → EAP triggered for remaining top 3.

--- page 5 ---
Narrative text, no figure. Early actions by priority impact: (1) Water Scarcity — WASH assessments, cash for water/WASH, repair water sources, install mass storage tanks; feasibility includes community acceptance and market assessments. (2) Lack of Pasture — fodder/feed distribution to livestock; proposed early actions: procure/target/distribute drought tolerant seeds, advocacy on pasture management/fodder preservation/packaging, provision of fodder for priority stock (commodity vouchers), market analysis, advocacy for expansion/protection of fallback grazing areas.

--- page 6 ---
Narrative text, no figure. Continued early actions: livestock market interventions (direct purchase of drought-affected animals considered but noted as difficult to scale); crop yield reduction addressed via drought tolerant seeds procurement and distribution — key constraint is seed quality/availability not cost. Cross-cutting: early warning information dissemination; framework agreements for purchases (pre-agreed quantities/specs/prices with suppliers); prepositioning of relief items.

--- page 7 ---
Narrative text, no figure. Enabling approaches: framework agreements for purchases; prepositioning; stable markets (FbA triggered by long-range drought forecasts — favourable rains can also cause problems if glut). Key activity: Pre-Crisis Market Analysis 1.5 months before season. EW dissemination as key early action activity. Preparedness includes: rapid purchase framework if quantities/specs agreed in advance; draft contracts for any items/transport; briefing of staff.

--- page 8 ---
Narrative text, no figure (continuation of page 7). Market-based interventions for alternative markets when production is high; activities include: Pre-Crisis Market Analysis (readiness activity, 1.5 months before season), linking producers to ready markets/suppliers, market-based interventions to stabilize markets.

--- page 9 ---
Planned operations table — Livelihoods sector. Target: 22,500 people (Female: 11,475; Male: 11,025). Budget: 109,774 CHF. AP Code: 007. Indicator: Number of people reached with livelihoods interventions in advance of a hazard. Pre-positioning (Year 1): Pre-agreement with suppliers (month 1). Early action activities (months 1–10): (1) Advocate for improved post-harvest crop management with seasonal county forecast dissemination (months 1–3); (2) Advocate for crop diversification with seasonal county forecast dissemination (months 1–2); (3) Procure, target and distribute drought tolerant seeds (months 1–2); (4) Advocate for expansion/protection of fallback grazing areas with seasonal county forecast (months 1–3); (5) Advocacy on pasture management/fodder preservation/packaging with seasonal county forecast (months 1–3); (6) Market Analysis (months 1–2); (7) Provision of fodder for priority stock (commodity vouchers) (months 1–2).

--- page 10 ---
Planned operations tables — two sectors. (A) Water Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH): Target: 7,500 people (Female: 3,825; Male: 3,675). Budget: 241,623 CHF. AP Code: 110, 111. Pre-positioning: Procurement of water pans/mass storage tanks (month 1); Agreement with service suppliers for WASH repair and rehabilitation (month 1). Early actions (months 1–3): CASH for WASH; Activation of water committees; Rapid assessment of water facility functionality; Repair and rehabilitation of water facilities; Desilting of water pans, repair of earth dams; Installation of mass water storage tanks. (B) Risk Reduction, Climate Adaptation and Recovery: Target: 150,000 people (Male: 73,500; Female: 76,500). Budget: 2,008 CHF. AP Code: 101, 103. Readiness: Compilation of existing EW templates and channels (month 1).

--- page 11 ---
Planned operations tables — two sectors. (A) Risk Reduction / EW (continuation): Early action activities: Dissemination of EW messages through 2 radios (months 1–2); Dissemination of EW messages through 150k Tera SMS (month 2). (B) Community Engagement and Accountability: Target: 150,000 people. Budget: 21,866 CHF. AP Code: 129. Readiness: Design and printing of stickers with KRCS community feedback mechanisms (month 1). Early actions: (1) Deploy trained volunteers to engage communities and support EW dissemination (months 1–6); (2) Hold community review meetings to review ongoing EAP implementation (months 1–6); (3) Community participatory documentation process (month 5). Enabling approach: Coordination and Partnerships — 24,097 CHF, AP Code: 049, 118, 119, 120, 121, 127, 128.

--- page 12 ---
Planned operations tables — three sectors. (A) Coordination and Partnerships (continued from p11): Early actions: post-trigger survey at 4 weeks (month 4); post-trigger survey at 8 weeks (month 8); after action review participatory approach (month 6); impact survey (month 7); lessons learned and after action review workshops with stakeholders (months 4 and 7). (B) Secretariat Services: Budget: 46,408 CHF. AP Code: 122. Readiness: IFRC Delegation provides technical accompaniment for annual readiness and pre-positioning activities (years 1–5). Early actions: IFRC support for early actions, monitoring, lessons learned and reporting (months 1–7). (C) National Society Strengthening: Budget: 53,424 CHF. AP Code: 105, 124, 125, 126.

--- page 13 ---
Planned operations table — National Society Strengthening (continuation). Indicator: At least 120 volunteers trained and insured to provide support to population at risk. Readiness: Focal Point for anticipatory action hired and leading implementation (years 1–5, months 1–5). Early actions: Insurance for 90 volunteers (months 1–3); Provide complete briefings on volunteers' roles and the risks they face (months 1–3).

--- page 14 ---
Budget table — EAP2022KN02, Kenya Red Cross Society Drought. Operating Budget: Readiness 85,107 / Pre-Pos Stock 46,855 / Early Action 367,238 / TOTAL 499,199 CHF. Planned Operations breakdown: Shelter and Basic Household Items 0/0/109,774/109,774; Livelihoods 0/0/109,774/109,774 [note: values partially merged in image]; Multi-purpose Cash 0/0/0/0; Health 0/0/0/0; Water, Sanitation & Hygiene 0/46,855/194,768/241,623; Protection, Gender and Inclusion 0/0/0/0; Education 0/0/0/0; Migration 0/0/0/0; Risk Red., Climate Adapt. and Recovery 0/0/2,008/2,008; Enabling Approaches total 80,644/0/43,285/123,929 (Coordination & Partnerships 24,097; Secretariat Services 46,408; National Society Strengthening 53,424; Community Engagement/Accountability 21,866). TOTAL BUDGET: 85,107 / 46,855 / 367,238 / 499,199 CHF. Contact info: Kenya Red Cross Secretary General Dr. Aicha Mohammed; IFRC Regional Office for Africa DM coordinator Patrick Elliott; IFRC Country Cluster Support Team Head Mohamed Babiker; IFRC DREF Senior Officer Nazira Lacayo.
